attributes.stem:
A 50-year-old man is being counselled about his options in terms of bariatric surgery. He has trialled but failed multiple attempts to reduce his BMI (body mass index) with diets and lifestyle modifications. His current BMI is 37kg/m2. His medical history includes hypertension, gastro-oesophageal reflux disease, obstructive sleep apnea and type 2 diabetes. His medications list includes lisinopril, amlodipine, rabeprazole, metformin and semaglutide. He is a non-smoker and consumes two to three standard drinks per week.

The different types of bariatric surgery including their benefits and side effects are discussed with him.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following would be the most appropriate management option for him?
options: He is not suitable for any elective surgery
attributes.explanation:
The indications for bariatric surgery are based on a person’s BMI (body mass index), which needs to be >40 or >35 with co-morbid conditions, such as type 2 diabetes, hypertension, hyperlipidaemia, obstructive sleep apnea, non-alcoholic fatty liver disease, among others. In his case he has a BMI of 37 plus co-morbid conditions of type 2 diabetes, hypertension and sleep apnea, making him a candidate for bariatric surgery. In addition to this the patient must have failed non-operative weight loss attempts, been cleared by a psychologist/psychiatrist and be able to give their informed consent.

In his case, despite the fact he has co-morbid conditions that do pose an anaesthetic risk he is still eligible to undergo an elective surgery after appropriate anaesthetic review. Hence he is a suitable candidate for elective surgery.

In regards to his co-morbid conditions his gastro-oesophageal reflux disease (GORD) is a large factor to play a role in which surgery is most appropriate for him.

Studies comparing the effect of different bariatric surgeries on gastro-oesophageal reflux disease (GORD) demonstrate that the Roux-en-Y gastric bypass was the best bariatric surgery in terms of reducing GORD symptoms, with a similar efficacy to the Nissen fundoplication - a surgical option to treat GORD. In terms of benefit for this patient, if he is wanting to undergo bariatric surgery it would make the greatest improvement if he underwent Roux-en-Y bypass as this would likely help his GORD symptoms as well as promote weight loss, compared to if he had the Nissen fundoplication, which would primarily improve his GORD symptoms, but may not promote as much weight loss.

Sleeve gastrectomy demonstrated a worsening of symptoms and increased prevalence of GORD and hence GORD is actually considered a contraindication to this type of procedure. Similarly, gastric banding initially showed improvement in GORD symptoms, but some patients experienced recurrence of symptoms, worsening of symptoms or oesophagitis in the long-term. Gastric ballooning has been demonstrated to result in worsening of GORD symptoms and can even result in the need for early removal due to these reflux symptoms. This has been put down to potentially the placement of the balloon with some positions resulting in less reports of reflux symptoms, but a Roux-en-Y would still be the preferred method if available for this patient.
learningPoints: Roux-en-Y gastric bypass is the best bariatric surgical option for patients with gastro-oesophageal reflux disease.
GORD is a contraindication to sleeve gastrectomy surgery.
Nissen fundoplication is a surgical option to treat GORD.
references: El-Hadi M, Birch DW, Gill RS, Karmali S. The effect of bariatric surgery on gastroesophageal reflux disease. Can J Surg. 2014;57(2):139-144. Available at: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3968207/
Ashrafi D, Osland E, Memon MA. Bariatric surgery and gastroesophageal reflux disease. Ann Transl Med. 2020;8(1):S11. Available at: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC7154328/

Barrichello S, Badurdeen D, Hedjoudje A, Neto M, 2020. The Effect of the Intra-gastric Balloon on Gastric Emptying and the DeMeester Score. Obesity Surgery. Available at: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/31254210/#:~:text=Conclusion%3A%20The%20IGB%20delays%20GE,a%20loss%20of%20LES%20pressure.
